frameworks.name: feature-specific vs feature-general interference in visual working memory
frameworks.one_line: Tests whether inter-item competition in WM depends on shared feature type.

frameworks.name: higher-order convergent WM storage models
frameworks.one_line: Supports models where capacity limits arise post-sensorily from convergent inputs.

frameworks.name: sensory vs post-sensory locus of WM capacity
frameworks.one_line: Contrasts feature-specific sensory interference with feature-general storage interference.

core_question: Does inter-item competition in visual working memory depend on the similarity of remembered features, and does this differ between encoding and maintenance?
key_assumptions: WM capacity is limited by inter-item competition
Precision indexes the strength of competition
manipulations: Homogeneous vs heterogeneous feature displays (e.g., colorful circles vs circles + oriented bars)
Retro-cue timing to dissociate encoding from online maintenance
key_findings: Heterogeneous displays yielded better WM precision than homogeneous ones
Retro-cueing revealed inter-item competition during storage was not feature-specific
interpretations: A feature-specific interference component operates during encoding
During maintenance, interference is feature-general, consistent with convergent higher-order storage
